title: Distributed Consensus (Paxos & Raft)
body:
# Distributed Consensus (Paxos & Raft)

## Hook — a motivating scenario

You run a "single" payment ledger on five machines for fault tolerance. Product demands: **exactly one
current ledger head**, it must **survive machine failures**, and there must be **no split-brain** (two
machines both think they're in charge, accepting conflicting writes). Those three demands — agree on
one value, tolerate failures, never disagree — are the problem of **distributed consensus**, and Paxos
and Raft are the algorithms that solve it.

## Mental model — make many machines agree on one ordered log

**Consensus** is getting a group of nodes to **agree on a single value** (or a single ordered sequence
of values) even though nodes can crash and messages can be delayed/lost. The dominant use is a
**replicated log**: all nodes agree on the same ordered list of commands and apply them in the same
order — turning N machines into one reliable **replicated state machine** (a database, a config store,
a lock service).

> Most "consensus systems" are really **replicated state machines** built on a consensus log: agree on
> log entry N before moving to N+1, and every replica applies the same commands in the same order.

## Build it up — safety first, then liveness; majorities

Two properties define correctness:
- **Safety ("nothing bad"):** never decide two different values for the same slot — no split-brain,
  ever. This is non-negotiable.
- **Liveness ("something good eventually"):** the system eventually makes progress (decides). Under bad
  conditions (partitions), consensus may pause progress to **preserve safety** — i.e. it chooses CP
  (recall CAP).

The key mechanism is the **majority quorum**: a decision needs votes from **more than half** the nodes.
Because any two majorities **overlap** in at least one node, two conflicting decisions can't both
gather a majority — that overlap is what guarantees safety. (5 nodes → need 3; tolerates 2 failures.)

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Why does requiring a majority quorum prevent split-brain, and how many failures does a 5-node cluster tolerate?",
  "answer": "Because any two majorities of the same set must share at least one member (two subsets each larger than half can't be disjoint). So if one partition/side tries to commit value A it needs a majority, and another side trying to commit value B also needs a majority — but they'd have to share a node, and that node won't vote for two conflicting decisions in the same slot. Hence at most one side can ever assemble a majority, making split-brain (two different committed values) impossible — that overlap is the heart of consensus safety. For a 5-node cluster, a majority is 3, so it can keep operating as long as 3 nodes are reachable, i.e. it tolerates 2 failures. If 3 or more fail (or a partition leaves every side with <3), no side has a majority, so the system stops making progress (loses liveness/availability) rather than risk two heads — preserving safety over availability (CP). In general N nodes tolerate floor((N-1)/2) failures, which is why consensus clusters use odd sizes (3, 5, 7)."
}
```

## Build it up — Paxos vs Raft

- **Paxos** (Lamport) was the original, proven-correct consensus algorithm — but notoriously hard to
  understand and implement; "Multi-Paxos" extends single decisions to a log.
- **Raft** was designed for **understandability** with the same guarantees. It decomposes consensus
  into: **leader election** (elect one leader per term), **log replication** (the leader takes client
  commands, appends to its log, replicates to followers; an entry is **committed** once a majority has
  it), and **safety** (rules ensuring an elected leader has all committed entries). One leader at a
  time means no conflicting writes; majority commit means no data loss on minority failure.

```sequence
{
  "title": "Raft log replication (commit on majority)",
  "actors": ["Client", "Leader", "Follower1", "Follower2"],
  "steps": [
    { "from": "Client", "to": "Leader", "label": "command X" },
    { "from": "Leader", "to": "Follower1", "label": "AppendEntries(X)" },
    { "from": "Leader", "to": "Follower2", "label": "AppendEntries(X)" },
    { "from": "Follower1", "to": "Leader", "label": "ack (now majority has X)" },
    { "from": "Leader", "to": "Client", "label": "committed → apply X to state machine" }
  ]
}
```

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "In Raft, why is a log entry only 'committed' once a majority of nodes have it — what would break if the leader committed after just writing locally?",
  "answer": "If a leader committed (and acknowledged to the client) an entry that only it had, then when that leader crashes, a new leader could be elected from the remaining nodes that never received the entry — and Raft's election only guarantees the new leader has entries present on a majority. The new leader would have a log missing that 'committed' entry, so it could overwrite that slot with a different command, violating safety: a committed value would be lost or two different values would exist for one slot (split-brain over time). By requiring a majority to have the entry before commit, Raft ensures any future leader (which must be elected by a majority, and Raft's voting rules require the candidate's log to be at least as up-to-date) necessarily includes that entry — because the committing majority and any electing majority overlap in at least one node that has it. So majority-commit + the up-to-date-log election rule together guarantee committed entries survive leader changes. Committing on a local write alone would make 'committed' meaningless across failures."
}
```

## In the wild

- **Consensus systems:** etcd and Consul (Raft), ZooKeeper (Zab, a leader-based atomic-broadcast
  protocol in the same family — and predating Raft), Google Chubby (Paxos) — used for **config, service
  discovery, leader election, and distributed locks**.
- **NewSQL databases** (Spanner, CockroachDB, YugabyteDB) use Paxos/Raft per shard to keep replicas
  strongly consistent (recall distributed databases).
- Consensus is **expensive** (a majority round trip per decision), so it's used for **metadata/control
  plane** and critical state, not every high-throughput data write — you put the bulk data elsewhere
  and use consensus for the parts that must agree.
- It underpins **leader election** and **distributed locks** (next chapters).

## Common misconception — "use consensus for everything to stay consistent / it makes you highly available"

Consensus is for the few things that must agree, and it trades availability for safety.

```reveal
{
  "prompt": "Why is running all your data writes through Raft/Paxos a mistake, and why doesn't consensus make a system 'more available'?",
  "answer": "Two reasons. Performance: every consensus decision requires a round trip to a majority of nodes (and a single leader serializes writes), so throughput is limited and latency is higher than a local or quorum write — fine for low-volume critical state (config, leadership, metadata, a ledger head) but a bottleneck if you funnel all high-throughput data through it. Real systems use consensus for the control plane / data that must strictly agree and keep bulk data in stores with cheaper replication (recall quorums/eventual consistency for AP workloads). Availability: consensus is a CP mechanism — to guarantee safety (never two values, no split-brain) it requires a majority to make progress, so when a majority isn't reachable (enough failures or a partition that leaves no side with a majority) it deliberately STOPS accepting writes rather than risk inconsistency. So consensus actually reduces availability under partition in exchange for correctness; it doesn't increase it. The right framing: use consensus precisely where you need strong agreement and can accept lower throughput and CP behavior, not as a blanket 'consistency switch' for everything."
}
```

Consensus (**Paxos/Raft**) makes nodes **agree on one ordered log** via **majority quorums**,
guaranteeing **safety (no split-brain) over liveness** (CP). It's powerful but **expensive** — use it
for **critical state/coordination**, not all data, and remember it pauses under partition to stay safe.

## Self-test

```quiz
{
  "question": "Distributed consensus algorithms like Paxos/Raft primarily let nodes:",
  "options": [
    "Cache data faster",
    "Agree on a single ordered log of values despite crashes/message loss (a replicated state machine)",
    "Avoid using a network",
    "Eliminate the need for replication"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "Consensus = agree on one value/ordered sequence; the common use is a replicated log so all nodes apply the same commands in order."
}
```

```quiz
{
  "question": "A 5-node Raft cluster needs a majority (3) to commit. This means it:",
  "options": [
    "Tolerates 4 node failures",
    "Tolerates 2 node failures, and stops making progress (not split-brain) if it can't reach a majority",
    "Never stops accepting writes",
    "Is always available under any partition"
  ],
  "answer": 1,
  "explanation": "Majority = 3 of 5 → tolerates 2 failures; without a majority it halts progress to preserve safety (CP), rather than risk two heads."
}
```

## Recap — key terms

Flip each card to check yourself, then move through the deck:

```flashcards
{ "title": "Distributed consensus — key terms", "cards": [
  { "front": "Consensus", "back": "Getting a group of nodes to agree on a single value (or one ordered sequence) despite crashes and delayed/lost messages." },
  { "front": "Replicated state machine", "back": "N machines agreeing on the same ordered log of commands and applying them in the same order, behaving like one reliable machine." },
  { "front": "Safety vs liveness", "back": "Safety = never decide two values for a slot (no split-brain), non-negotiable. Liveness = eventually make progress; consensus pauses it to keep safety (CP)." },
  { "front": "Majority quorum", "back": "A decision needs votes from more than half the nodes. Any two majorities overlap in at least one node, so conflicting decisions can't both win — guaranteeing safety." },
  { "front": "Failure tolerance", "back": "N nodes tolerate ⌊(N−1)/2⌋ failures; a 5-node cluster needs 3 and tolerates 2. Without a majority it halts progress. Use odd sizes." },
  { "front": "Paxos vs Raft", "back": "Both give the same guarantees. Paxos (Lamport) is original but hard to understand; Raft is designed for understandability via leader election, log replication, and safety rules." }
] }
```

## Key takeaways

- **Consensus** makes crash-prone nodes **agree on one ordered log** → a **replicated state machine**
  (config stores, locks, ledgers, NewSQL shards).
- **Safety (never two values / no split-brain) is non-negotiable**; under partition, consensus pauses
  **liveness** to keep safety (**CP**).
- **Majority quorums** guarantee safety because any two majorities **overlap**; N nodes tolerate
  ⌊(N−1)/2⌋ failures (use odd sizes).
- **Raft** (understandable) and **Paxos** give the same guarantees; consensus is **expensive** — use it
  for **critical state/coordination**, not every write.

## Up next

A primary use of consensus is choosing who's in charge. Next: **Leader Election**.
